narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 60% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in academic records, test scores, and backgrounds of past students, focusing on factors like grades, LSAT performance, undergraduate GPA, class rankings, and family income without considering gender or race. In your case, three key elements stood out as the strongest drivers of this outcome: your LSAT score, undergraduate GPA, and Year 3 class decile ranking. Your LSAT score was the most influential, working strongly in your favor by suggesting a solid potential for bar success, as it exceeds the average among students who passed. However, your undergraduate GPA pushed the prediction toward failure, landing below the typical 3.24 average for bar passers and signaling potential challenges in sustaining high performance. Similarly, your Year 3 decile of 3 contributed toward the failure prediction, falling short of the around 6th decile average for those who succeeded, indicating your school ranking there raised concerns about readiness. While your middle family income aligns with over a third of bar passers and your full-time status matches nearly all of them, these didn't outweigh the drags from GPA and decile. Compared to typical accepted students, who often combine stronger GPAs and higher deciles with even average LSATs, your profile showed promise in testing but gaps in prior academic consistency that tipped the scales. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's a data-driven risk assessment to ensure we admit those with the highest predicted odds of bar success. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT if possible to build on your strength there, bolstering your undergraduate record through post-bac courses or a master's program to lift your GPA perception, and targeting schools where your full-time commitment and middle-income stability are bigger assets. Many students in similar positions improve these metrics and gain admission elsewhere—reach out to advisors for personalized strategies, and keep pursuing your goals with persistence.
